why needed for 202505 branch:
The image builds in 202505 sometimes fail due to network access to archive.ubuntu.com:80 issue. 
```
W: Failed to fetch http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/dists/focal/InRelease  Connection failed [IP: 185.125.190.81 80]
W: Failed to fetch http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/dists/focal-updates/InRelease  Connection failed [IP: 185.125.190.82 80]
W: Failed to fetch http://archive.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/dists/focal-backports/InRelease  Connection failed [IP: 91.189.91.81 80]
W: Failed to fetch http://security.ubuntu.com/ubuntu/dists/focal-security/InRelease  Connection failed [IP: 91.189.91.81 80]
W: Some index files failed to download. They have been ignored, or old ones used instead.
```
With this change, we can use our debian mirror for a more stable connection.

@deepak-singhal0408, can we introduce a common file add the soc properties for all dnx platforms ?

@arlakshm — thanks for the suggestion. I investigated the existing common config infrastructure to assess feasibility.

### What exists today

The per-chip-family common config mechanism at `device/broadcom/x86_64-broadcom_common/` works well for XGS (TD2/TD3/TD4/TH*). The merge logic in `config_syncd_bcm()` ([syncd_init_common.sh](https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-sairedis/blob/master/syncd/scripts/syncd_init_common.sh#L265)) already has a YML/DNX code path that reads chip IDs from `/proc/linux_ngbde` — so the sonic-sairedis side is ready.

### What's missing for DNX

The plumbing isn't wired up on the sonic-buildimage side:
1. **Volume mount** — `docker-syncd-brcm-dnx.mk` doesn't mount `x86_64-broadcom_common` (unlike `docker-syncd-brcm.mk` [which does](https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-buildimage/blob/master/platform/broadcom/docker-syncd-brcm.mk#L18))
2. **Chip directories** — no common config dirs exist for DNX chip IDs (J2, J2C+, J2P, Q3D)
3. **Platform opt-in** — no `common_config_support` flag files on any DNX platform

### Effort to enable it

It's a sonic-buildimage-only change (~20-25 files):
- 1-line volume mount addition in `docker-syncd-brcm-dnx.mk`
- ~3-4 new chip-family directories with `.bcm` files using `[Low Inheritance Precedence]`
- ~15 empty `common_config_support` flag files across DNX platforms

However, the **testing burden is significantly higher**:
- Chip ID mapping (first 3 hex chars from `/proc/linux_ngbde`) must be determined on **real hardware** for each DNX chip family (J2, J2C+, J2P, Q3D) — can't be tested in KVM/VS
- All 3 vendors (Arista, Nokia, NextHop) would ideally validate the merge behavior on their platforms
- Some platforms use `.yml` configs vs `.bcm`, so both merge paths need verification

### Proposal

Can we land this PR as-is? It's a single well-tested SOC property (`sai_ecmp_group_members_increment=8`) across 25 files, validated on real J2C+ and Q3D hardware. The direct approach has near-zero regression risk (additive append, no runtime merge logic involved).

I'm happy to take on enabling the common config framework for DNX as a follow-up PR — it would be a great foundation for future DNX-wide SOC properties. Once that's in place, this property can be consolidated into the common files.

## 🔒 Trivy Security Scan Results — `docker-sonic-mgmt:latest`

**Scan date**: 2026-04-10 | **Image**: `docker-sonic-mgmt:latest` (Ubuntu 24.04) | **Docker CE**: 29.4.0
**Scan command**: `trivy image docker-sonic-mgmt:latest --scanners vuln --ignore-unfixed`

### What this PR fixes

| CVE | Severity | Package | Before | After | Fix |
|-----|----------|---------|--------|-------|-----|
| CVE-2025-4565 | **HIGH** | protobuf | 4.24.4 | 7.34.1 | Force-upgrade after batch pip install |
| CVE-2026-0994 | **HIGH** | protobuf | 4.24.4 | 7.34.1 | Same |
| CVE-2026-39892 | MEDIUM | cryptography | 46.0.6 | 46.0.7 | Patch Azure CLI bundled `/opt/az` + remove stale metadata |
| CVE-2025-8869 | MEDIUM | pip | 24.0 | 26.0.1 | `pip install --upgrade pip` |
| OS packages | various | libssl, etc. | unpatched | patched | `apt-get upgrade -y` |

### Changes made
1. **`apt-get upgrade -y`** — upgrades all vulnerable OS packages (same pattern as docker-ptf)
2. **`pip install --upgrade pip`** — fixes CVE-2025-8869
3. **`cryptography>=46.0.7`** — pinned in pip install + patched Azure CLI's bundled copy
4. **`protobuf>=6.33.5`** — pinned in pip install + force-upgraded separately (batch install constrains to 4.x due to opentelemetry deps)

### Remaining vulnerabilities (unfixable)

#### ansible 11.10.0 → 12.2.0 (MEDIUM, SKIP)
- Ansible 12.x is a **major version upgrade** (ansible-core 2.18 → 2.19)
- The Dockerfile patches `ansible/plugins/loader.py` — this patch will not apply cleanly on ansible 12.x
- sonic-mgmt playbooks and roles need separate validation for ansible-core 2.19 compatibility
- **Decision**: Skip — requires dedicated validation effort, not safe for a security-only PR

#### Docker binaries — docker-buildx, docker-compose (4 HIGH, upstream)

| CVE | Severity | Package | Installed | Fix | Binary |
|-----|----------|---------|-----------|-----|--------|
| CVE-2026-34040 | HIGH | docker/docker | v28.5.2 | 29.3.1 | buildx + compose |
| CVE-2026-39883 | HIGH | otel/sdk | v1.40.0–v1.42.0 | 1.43.0 | buildx + compose |
| + 6 MEDIUM | MEDIUM | stdlib | v1.25.8 | 1.25.9+ | docker + buildx + compose |

- **docker-ce-cli 29.4.0** is already the latest from Docker's apt repo
- **docker-buildx-plugin 0.33.0** and **docker-compose-plugin 5.1.2** are the latest available
- These are pre-built Go binaries shipped by Docker Inc — vulns are baked in upstream
- **Decision**: Upstream issue — Docker Inc needs to rebuild with Go 1.26.2+ and otel 1.43.0+

### Summary

| | Before | After | Delta |
|--|--------|-------|-------|
| Python HIGH | 2 | **0** ✅ | -2 |
| Python MEDIUM | 3 | **1** | -2 |
| Go (upstream) | 29 | 29 | unfixable |
| **Total** | **35** | **30** | **-5 fixed** |

> (I was trying to expidite the code review so you can merge critical 
> code change sooner. If the change is purely a refactoring, I can 
> easily approve this PR. If you want to mix into this PR, its okay.)
>
> Nested |uses| resolution only works when the parent already has 
> *multiple* |uses| clauses.
>
> When a grouping contains a nested |uses|, 
> |_compileUsesClauseList(model, groupdata, 'uses', ...)| appends it to 
> |model['uses']| /during/ iteration of |uses_s|. This works in 
> |test-grouping.yang| because |TEST_GROUPING_LIST| has 3 |uses| → 
> xmltodict produces a *list*, |uses_s| aliases that same list object, 
> and the appended item gets picked up by the |for uses in uses_s:| loop.
>
> But if a node has a *single* |uses nested-uses-group;|:
>
>   * xmltodict produces a dict for |model["uses"]|
>   * |uses_s = [uses_s]| creates a /new/ local list
>   * |_compileUsesClauseList| does |model['uses'] = [model['uses']]|
>     (another new list) and appends the nested |uses| there
>   * the |for uses in uses_s:| loop never sees it
>   * |del model["uses"]| then drops it unresolved
>
> *Suggested fix* — normalize |model["uses"]| to a list /before/ 
> capturing |uses_s| so they alias the same object:
>
> uses_s = model.get("uses")
> if not uses_s:
>      return
> if isinstance(uses_s,dict):
>      model["uses"]= [uses_s]
>      uses_s = model["uses"]
>
> (or alternatively re-loop while |model.get("uses")| is non-empty)
>
> Also worth adding a test case in |test-grouping.yang| with a container 
> that has a /single/ |uses| referencing a grouping that itself contains 
> |uses|, to cover this path.

Update: Verified fix in community virtual switch image.

Built two VS images from the PR branch — one at the base commit (before fix, `b55c142e4`) and one at the PR HEAD (after fix, `6f06ea8c8`). On each image, configured two interfaces with IPs, added a static route overlapping the connected route's prefix, then shut down the connected route's interface to trigger the failover to the static route.

#### swss.rec logs

Before fix (`b55c142e4`):
```
2026-04-06.20:21:39.787698|ROUTE_TABLE:10.10.0.0/24|SET|protocol:kernel|nexthop:0.0.0.0|ifname:Ethernet0|weight:1
2026-04-06.20:22:06.939803|ROUTE_TABLE:10.10.0.0/24|SET|protocol:kernel|nexthop:0.0.0.0|ifname:Ethernet0|weight:1
2026-04-06.20:22:43.847302|ROUTE_TABLE:10.10.0.0/24|DEL
```

The last event for `10.10.0.0/24` is a DEL. There is no subsequent SET for the static route. The MRIB route deletion wiped the prefix from APP_DB. The static route update from zebra never made it.

After fix (`6f06ea8c8`):
```
2026-04-06.20:21:42.093099|ROUTE_TABLE:10.10.0.0/24|SET|protocol:kernel|nexthop:0.0.0.0|ifname:Ethernet0|weight:1
2026-04-06.20:22:09.138771|ROUTE_TABLE:10.10.0.0/24|SET|protocol:kernel|nexthop:0.0.0.0|ifname:Ethernet0|weight:1
2026-04-06.20:22:46.474887|ROUTE_TABLE:10.10.0.0/24|SET|protocol:0xc4|nexthop:20.0.0.2|ifname:Ethernet4|weight:1
```

The last event is a SET with `protocol:0xc4` (static route), nexthop `20.0.0.2` via `Ethernet4`. The MRIB delete was filtered out by the fix, so it no longer clobbers the prefix.


#### Full Details / Logs

<details>
<summary>Details (Image Build Settings and Testing Setup)</summary>

Run this separately in two directories, build for:
- Current PR HEAD with fix, https://github.com/Bojun-Feng/sonic-buildimage/commit/6f06ea8c83cc00464d1972cbd2d217fcbb14f1e0
- Current PR base commit (HEAD~1) before fix, https://github.com/Bojun-Feng/sonic-buildimage/commit/b55c142e407140eac8c3f3c9bae55af2bd8fa5fe

```bash
# Current PR HEAD with fix
git clone https://github.com/Bojun-Feng/sonic-buildimage
cd sonic-buildimage
git checkout bug/mrib-route-fib-25173
git checkout 6f06ea8c83cc00464d1972cbd2d217fcbb14f1e0

# Current PR base commit (HEAD~1) before fix
git clone https://github.com/Bojun-Feng/sonic-buildimage
cd sonic-buildimage
git checkout bug/mrib-route-fib-25173
git checkout b55c142e407140eac8c3f3c9bae55af2bd8fa5fe
```

Then run same build commands in both directories:
```bash
git submodule update --init --recursive

# Configure Image, wrapped in logging util
mkdir -p "$HOME/sonic_logs" && \
ts=$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S) && \
{
  make init &&  \
  NOBUSTER=1 NOBULLSEYE=1 make configure \
    PLATFORM=vs
} 2>&1 | tee \
  "sonic_configure_$ts.log" \
  "$HOME/sonic_logs/sonic_configure_$ts.log"

# Build Image, wrapped in logging util
mkdir -p "$HOME/sonic_logs" && \
ts=$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S) && \
{
  NOBUSTER=1 NOBULLSEYE=1 make \
    SONIC_BUILD_JOBS=4 \
    SONIC_BUILD_RETRY_COUNT=1 \
    target/sonic-vs.img.gz 
} 2>&1 | tee \
  "sonic_build_$ts.log" \
  "$HOME/sonic_logs/sonic_build_$ts.log"
```

After that, run the two virtual switches in GNS3, no need to connect them to anything. Use web console to run the commands listed in https://github.com/sonic-net/sonic-buildimage/issues/25173#issuecomment-4186559462, starting with bug replication commands, followed by redis-cli commands and finally `show version` to verify the fix.

Note I encountered some DASH error messages during build related to the lack of Trixie support, but the `sonic-vs.img.gz` files were built successfully.
</details>

## Code Review

### High

1. **`cli/clear/plugins/clear_dhcp_relay.py` — `SUPPORTED_DHCP_TYPE`**: Adding "Malformed" and "Ignored" is a **silent behavioral change for v4 clear**. Previously `clear dhcp_relay ipv4 counters` did not zero these; now it does. If operators rely on accumulated Malformed/Ignored counts for diagnostics, this clears data they may need. Please document this as a breaking change and confirm it's intentional.

2. **`cli/show/plugins/show_dhcp_relay.py` — `dhcp_relay_ip6counters`**: **Breaking change**: `show dhcp_relay ipv6 counters` now reads dhcpmon COUNTERS_DB instead of dhcp6relay STATE_DB. If dhcpmon v6 isn't deployed, this command returns nothing where it previously showed dhcp6relay counters. The old behavior is only accessible via `show dhcp6relay_counters counts`. This should be explicitly documented as a migration in the changelog and release notes.

### Medium

3. **`cli/clear/plugins/clear_dhcp_relay.py` — `notify_dhcpmon_processes`**: Sends SIGUSR1/SIGUSR2 to **all** dhcpmon processes regardless of v4/v6. A v4-only dhcpmon instance will unnecessarily pause and re-sync when clearing v6 counters. Consider filtering by the process command line (`-4`/`-6` flag) to scope the signal.

4. **`cli/show/plugins/show_dhcp_relay.py`**: Display types intentionally exclude "Malformed"/"Ignored" but the `clear` command now zeros them. Users can clear fields they can never see via `show`. Consider adding `--verbose` or `--all` flag to `show`, or at minimum document this asymmetry.

5. **`cli-plugin-tests/test_clear_dhcp_relay.py` — `test_clear_dhcpmon_db_counters`**: Only tested with v4 constants (`DHCPV4_COUNTER_TABLE_PREFIX`, `SUPPORTED_DHCP_TYPE`). Since this is now a shared function, it should also be parameterized to test with v6 constants (`DHCPV6_COUNTER_TABLE_PREFIX`, `SUPPORTED_DHCPV6_TYPE`).

6. **`cli-plugin-tests/test_clear_dhcp_relay.py` — `test_clear_dhcp_relay_ipv6_counters`**: The assertion was weakened — only checks the 2nd positional arg. Does NOT verify v6-specific constants (lock file, state table) are passed correctly. A regression where v4 constants are accidentally used for v6 would go undetected.

### Low

7. **`cli/show/plugins/show_dhcp_relay.py` — `get_dhcpmon_vlans`**: `db.keys()` can return `None` in some SonicV2Connector implementations. `for key in None` → `TypeError`. Adding `or []` guard is a one-line fix. Same applies to `clear_dhcpmon_db_counters`.

8. **`cli/clear/plugins/clear_dhcp_relay.py` — `clear_dhcpmon_counters` finally block**: `fcntl.flock(lock_file, LOCK_UN)` is called unconditionally even if locking failed. On Linux this is a no-op but semantically incorrect. Gate on `locked`.

---

Overall the refactoring from duplicated v4/v6 functions into shared parameterized versions is clean. The CLI wiring is correct. **Items 1–2 are the most important** — the behavioral changes on existing commands should be clearly documented to avoid surprising operators during upgrade.

> ## Code Review
> This PR is a cleanup/refactor for the Aspeed BMC platform in SONiC. Here's a breakdown:
> 
> ### Changes Summary
> **1. Platform services → proper Debian package** ✅ Good
> 
> * Moves scripts/services from ad-hoc `build_debian.sh` installation into a proper `aspeed-platform-services` Debian package (`.mk`, `debian/` scaffolding, `rules.mk`, `one-image.mk`)
> * This is the right pattern — proper packaging vs. raw file copies in build scripts
> 
> **2. New watchdog keepalive daemon** (`watchdog-keepalive.sh`)
> 
> * 180s timeout, 60s keepalive interval
> * Custom log rotation (5 files max)
> * Uses `watchdogutil arm/disarm` for setup/teardown
> 
> **3. Watchdog platform API refactor** (both AST2700 EVB and NextHop)
> 
> * Removes `self.armed` tracking in favor of sysfs state (`is_armed()` reads sysfs `state`)
> * Removes `_close_watchdog()` (magic 'V' close) — replaces with `_disablewatchdog()` using ioctl `WDIOS_DISABLECARD`
> * Adds proper ioctl methods: `_enablewatchdog`, `_keepalive`, `_settimeout`, `_gettimeout`, `_gettimeleft`
> * `arm()` now caps at 300s max — is this intentional? Some use cases may need longer timeouts
> 
> **4. Reboot cause logic improvement** (both chassis.py files)
> 
> * `WDIOF_CARDRESET` now checks for software reboot cause file before defaulting to watchdog
> * Previously always returned `NON_HARDWARE`, now returns `WATCHDOG` when no software cause found — good fix
> 
> **5. Removed BMC component from platform.json** (both EVB and NextHop)
> 
> * Removes "BMC" from components list — why? Is BMC FW update not supported via `fwutil`?

This platform itself is a BMC and makes little sense to have a BMC component within it was the point Judy made in the code review. That made sense and hence removed it.

> 
> **6. Removed obmc-console configs** (both EVB and NextHop)
> 
> * Deleted `server.tty.conf` files and related install logic — are these now managed elsewhere (OpenBMC side)?

We no longer use obmc-console, instead we have moved to use SONiC's native consutil service and hence removed this.

> 
> **7. USB network init fix** (NextHop)
> 
> * Hardcodes UDC name to `12021000.usb-vhub:p1` instead of auto-detecting first available — could this break on different AST2700 boards?

This is under the Nexthop platform module and is specific to our card. Will not affect the rest.

> 
> **8. New eMMC installer script** (`install-sonic-to-emmc.sh`)
> 
> * Installs SONiC from OpenBMC to eMMC with U-Boot env setup
> * Has commented-out alternatives and hardcoded `BOOTCONF="nexthop-b27-r0"` — should these be configurable rather than requiring source edits?

Fair point. As these scripts are helper scripts (not part of the image build) I didn't make them configurable. If we really need that I can make it as such. Do let me know.

> 
> ### Issues
> **1. Duplicate code across EVB and NextHop**
> 
> * `chassis.py` reboot cause changes are identical in both `ast2700/` and `nexthop/common/`
> * `watchdog.py` changes are identical in both
> * Should this be a shared base class?

If we make this a shared base class, then we will need a new intermediate python wheel package. Also, chassis.py will change for nexthop card to accommodate eeprom and other features. As of today there are same, but expected to diverge.

> 
> **2. Commented-out code left in**
> 
> * `build-emmc-image-installer.sh`: `#EMMC_SIZE_MB=7168`
> * `install-sonic-to-emmc.sh`: `#BOOTCONF="ast2700-evb"`
> * These should be removed or made configurable

Eval board ships with a 8G eMMC whereas Nexthop card will ship with a 32G (but 10G due to pSLC). To catrer
to both these cards, I just added lines which can be uncommented depending on which cards these scripts target.
I thought as these are helper scripts, there might be more additions depending vendor requirements. So instead of making this configurable I just added comments. I can fix them if you insist.
> 
> **3. `watchdog-keepalive.sh` logs every 60s**
> 
> * `echo "Watchdog keepalive sent"` on every iteration generates ~1440 log lines/day — consider removing or making it periodic (every Nth keepalive)
> 
Thanks for pointing out, Will fix.

> **4. Deleted `ast2700/setup.py`** — is the platform wheel now built differently, or was this unused?

yes it was unused.

> 
> **5. PR title is vague** — "Address pending review comments and some cleanup" doesn't convey the scope. This has significant changes (new Debian package, watchdog refactor, reboot cause fix, eMMC installer).

Will fix the title.

I think I'm missing something: if, at runtime, `resolv-config.sh` is recreating the symlink, and is running `resolvconf -u`, then why do some containers still not have a properly-populated `/etc/resolv.conf`?

Because `update.d/libc` has two sequential gate checks with a file write between them.
The two gates are satisfied at different times but never in the same invocation — and the intermediate write permanently prevents the second invocation from reaching Gate 2.

The relevant code is in [`/etc/resolvconf/update.d/libc`](https://sources.debian.org/src/resolvconf/1.94/etc/resolvconf/update.d/libc) (resolvconf 1.94, Trixie). The execution order of the three critical sections:

1. **[L142-146](https://sources.debian.org/src/resolvconf/1.94/etc/resolvconf/update.d/libc#L142-146) — Gate 1: content-unchanged check**
   ```sh
   if [ -f "$DYNAMICRSLVCNFFILE" ] && [ "$(cat $TMPFILE)" = "$(cat $DYNAMICRSLVCNFFILE)" ] ; then
       rm -f "$TMPFILE"
       exit 0
   fi
   ```
   Compares newly generated content (`$TMPFILE`) with the existing `/run/resolvconf/resolv.conf`.
   If identical, exits.

2. **[L149](https://sources.debian.org/src/resolvconf/1.94/etc/resolvconf/update.d/libc#L149) — File write (between the two gates)**
   ```sh
   mv -f "$TMPFILE" "$DYNAMICRSLVCNFFILE"
   ```
   Persists the new content to `/run/resolvconf/resolv.conf` **before** the symlink check.
   Once written, all future invocations will see "unchanged" at Gate 1.

3. **[L153](https://sources.debian.org/src/resolvconf/1.94/etc/resolvconf/update.d/libc#L153) — Gate 2: symlink check**
   ```sh
   resolv_conf_is_symlinked_to_dynamic_file || exit 0
   ```
   Where [`resolv_conf_is_symlinked_to_dynamic_file`](https://sources.debian.org/src/resolvconf/1.94/etc/resolvconf/update.d/libc#L60) is:
   ```sh
   [ -L ${ETC}/resolv.conf ] && [ "$(readlink -m ${ETC}/resolv.conf)" = "$DYNAMICRSLVCNFFILE" ]
   ```
   If `/etc/resolv.conf` is not a symlink to `/run/resolvconf/resolv.conf`, exits without
   running `update-libc.d/` (which contains `update-containers`).

The ordering of L149 before L153 is the root of the deadlock: a Gate 2 failure commits the
content to disk, making any subsequent invocation fail at Gate 1 regardless of whether the
symlink has since been created.


### Containers start before DHCP — all read empty `/etc/resolv.conf`

```
21:16:31.356832  resolv-config: [update-containers:main] Updating resolv.conf for container database
21:16:37.943833  resolv-config: [update-containers:main] Updating resolv.conf for container restapi
21:16:39.007647  resolv-config: [update-containers:main] Updating resolv.conf for container swss
......
```

Each of these is `postStartAction` calling `update-containers $DOCKERNAME`. The script does
`RESOLV_CONTENT=$(cat /etc/resolv.conf)` — but `/etc/resolv.conf` is a regular empty file
(from `build_debian.sh`'s `touch`), so every container gets empty DNS written into it.

10 containers running with empty DNS. DHCP hasn't completed yet. The
`libc` → `update-containers` bulk update is supposed to fix them once DHCP finishes.


### DHCP completes at 21:16:53 — bulk update blocked by Gate 2

```
21:16:53.379431  dhclient[9279]: DHCPDISCOVER on eth0 to 255.255.255.255 port 67 interval 8
21:16:53.381622  dhclient[9279]: DHCPOFFER of 10.210.25.14 from 10.222.36.70
21:16:53.381658  dhclient[9279]: DHCPREQUEST for 10.210.25.14 on eth0 to 255.255.255.255 port 67
21:16:53.384519  dhclient[9279]: DHCPACK of 10.210.25.14 from 10.222.36.70
21:16:53.453073  dhclient[9279]: bound to 10.210.25.14 -- renewal in 10641 seconds.
```

dhclient hook calls `resolvconf -a eth0.dhclient` → triggers `update.d/libc`:

| Step | What happens | State |
|---|---|---|

## Review

Good security improvement — removes `--no-check-certificate` and adds SHA256 integrity verification. A few suggestions:

**1. Agree with @yejianquan: move `IFUPDOWN2_SHA256` to `rules/ifupdown2.mk`**

`IFUPDOWN2_VERSION` is defined there, and the SHA256 is tightly coupled to it. When someone bumps the version, they'll miss updating the hash if it lives in a different file. Suggest:

`makefile
# rules/ifupdown2.mk
IFUPDOWN2_VERSION = 3.0.0-1
IFUPDOWN2_SHA256 = 55ded1c60f720c81a5d4b5feeb48d893fd3f29d4ad0fe7d9cec508fe84b5f1b8
export IFUPDOWN2_VERSION IFUPDOWN2_SHA256
`

**2. The `-O` flag addition is correct** — without it, wget follows GitHub's CDN redirect and may save with an unexpected filename, causing the `sha256sum` check to fail.

**3. Minor: consider cleanup on hash mismatch** — if `sha256sum -c` fails, the corrupted tarball remains on disk. The `rm -rf` at the top of the recipe handles re-runs, so this is not a blocker, but worth noting.

Overall LGTM with the suggestion to move the hash constant.

## CI Failure Analysis: `syslog/test_logrotate.py` on t0 and multi-asic-t1

### Root Cause

`test_logrotate.py` mounts a small loopback device over `/var/log`, then calls `config_reload()`. During config_reload, `rsyslog-config.sh` runs:

- **Before this PR**: Always runs `systemctl restart rsyslog` → rsyslog re-opens `/var/log/syslog` on the new mount ✅
- **After this PR**: Skips restart if config unchanged → rsyslog keeps writing to the old file descriptor (hidden under the new mount) → test sees empty/stale `/var/log/syslog` ❌

The `cmp` optimization is correct for the config-only case, but breaks when the filesystem has changed underneath rsyslog.

### Suggested Fix

Instead of skipping rsyslog entirely when config is unchanged, use `systemctl reload rsyslog` (sends SIGHUP):

```bash
if ! cmp -s "$TMPFILE" /etc/rsyslog.conf 2>/dev/null; then
    cp "$TMPFILE" /etc/rsyslog.conf || { echo "Failed to update /etc/rsyslog.conf" >&2; exit 1; }
    systemctl restart rsyslog
else
    # Config unchanged, but rsyslog may need to re-open log files
    # (e.g., after /var/log remount). Reload is fast (~0ms vs ~4s restart on Trixie).
    systemctl reload rsyslog
fi
```

This preserves the Trixie performance fix (no 4s restart delay) while ensuring rsyslog always re-reads its config and re-opens file handles.

### Other Failures

The other 4 failed jobs (t1-lag, dualtor, t2, vpp) are all `LOCK_TESTBED_FAILED` — infra/resource contention, unrelated to this PR.

Fix pushed in c3ae8cc5c: replaced the skip-if-unchanged approach with `reload-or-restart` + an `ExecReload` systemd drop-in.

**Root cause of test_logrotate.py failure:** The previous approach skipped rsyslog restart when the config file was unchanged. But `test_logrotate.py` remounts `/var/log` to a different device — rsyslog still has file descriptors pointing to the old filesystem. It needs a restart/reload regardless of config changes.

**Fix:**
- Always write config and call `systemctl reload-or-restart rsyslog`
- Add `/etc/systemd/system/rsyslog.service.d/reload.conf` with `ExecReload=/bin/kill -HUP $MAINPID` so rsyslog can do in-place config reload via SIGHUP
- On systems without the drop-in, `reload-or-restart` falls back to `restart` (same as before)
- On Trixie with the drop-in, rsyslog reloads in-place with zero downtime

### Root cause analysis of CI failures

All 3 KVM test topologies (t0, t1-lag, t2) failed on **`syslog/test_logrotate.py`** (2 test functions each).

**Root cause:** The previous conditional-restart approach (`cmp -s` + skip) broke tests that call `config_reload` after filesystem changes:

1. `test_logrotate_small_size` mounts a new filesystem on `/var/log`
2. Calls `config_reload` → triggers `rsyslog-config.sh`
3. Old code: skip restart because config unchanged
4. rsyslog keeps stale file descriptors to the old `/var/log` mount
5. New `/var/log/syslog` never gets written → logrotate assertions fail

Same issue affects `test_logrotate_full_partition_no_archives_cleanup`.

**Fix:** Completely reworked the approach. Instead of skipping the restart (working around the symptom), fix the root cause:

- **Trixie's rsyslog.service** has `PrivateTmp=yes`, `ProtectSystem=full`, `ProtectKernelTunables=yes`, etc.
- systemd sets up and tears down mount/PID/network namespaces on every start/stop — that's the ~4s overhead
- Added a **systemd drop-in override** that resets all sandboxing directives to defaults (disabled)
- rsyslog-config.sh is **reverted to original behavior** (always restart)

This is the correct fix because SONiC runs rsyslog as root on the host — the sandboxing adds no practical security value but costs 4 seconds per restart.
